objections.description: The spec is literally truncated mid-sentence. The critical-path theme initialization script in <head> is incomplete — this is the single most important piece for preventing a dark-to-light flash on every page load. Without this, every light-mode user sees a full FOUC on every navigation. Shipping this spec as-is means shipping a broken theme system.
objections.suggested_fix: Complete the inline script: read localStorage, fallback to prefers-color-scheme, set data-theme attribute on <html> synchronously before any CSS paints. Full implementation: `(function(){var t=localStorage.getItem('app-theme');if(!t)t=matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme:light)').matches?'light':'dark';document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme',t)})();` Also add a `<style>html{visibility:hidden}html[data-theme]{visibility:visible}</style>` guard if the script somehow fails.

objections.location: Section 1 — Build System: import map + esbuild --splitting
objections.description: esbuild --splitting with --format=esm produces bare-specifier imports between chunks (e.g., `import {x} from './chunk-ABC.js'`). But the spec also says React is loaded via import maps from vendor/. Import maps and esbuild's module resolution are two different systems. If esbuild bundles React into the output (which it will unless marked external), the import map is dead code. If React is marked external, esbuild emits `import 'react'` which the import map resolves — but only in browsers that support import maps (Safari 16.4+, no Firefox ESR). This is an unresolved contradiction that will either double-bundle React or break in older browsers.
objections.suggested_fix: Pick one strategy: (A) Mark react/react-dom as external in esbuild, rely on import maps, and document the browser support floor as Safari 16.4+ / Chrome 89+ / Firefox 108+. Add the es-module-shims polyfill (~4KB) as a fallback for older browsers. OR (B) Let esbuild bundle React into the output, remove the vendor/ directory and import map entirely. I recommend (B) — simpler, zero compatibility risk, and the bundle size difference is negligible since React is in the output either way.

objections.location: Section 5 — API Contract: missing backend endpoint verification
objections.description: The spec defines 8 API endpoints (GET /api/metrics, GET /api/status, etc.) but the migration path never verifies these endpoints exist in web_server.py. The user's constraint says 'preserve backend endpoints' — which implies there ARE existing endpoints, but we don't know if they match this contract. If the backend serves /api/v1/search instead of /api/search, the entire frontend is dead on arrival. This is a deploy-time landmine.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a Section 0 or pre-migration step: 'Audit web_server.py routes. Map existing endpoint paths to api.js fetch functions. If endpoint names differ, api.js adapts to the actual backend — NOT the other way around. Document exact endpoint mapping before writing any fetch code.' Also add a runtime check: api.js should log or surface a clear error when it hits a 404, not silently fail into the generic error state.

objections.location: Section 6 — State: useFetch custom hook with stale-while-revalidate
objections.description: Rolling your own SWR with a stale-time cache inside a useReducer context is the #1 source of subtle bugs in custom React data layers. Race conditions between stale reads and concurrent refetches, cache invalidation on mutations (executeAction), stale closures over AbortControllers — these are not theoretical, they're guaranteed to happen. The spec says 'no external library' but then re-implements 60% of React Query poorly.
objections.suggested_fix: Two options: (A) Accept the complexity budget and spec out the exact cache invalidation rules — what happens to cached timeline data after executeAction? What happens when two components mount simultaneously and both trigger the same fetch? Document the concurrency model explicitly. OR (B) Drop stale-while-revalidate entirely. Just fetch-on-mount with AbortController cleanup in useEffect. Simpler, correct, and the 30-second stale window barely matters for a v1 where users aren't rapidly switching tabs. I strongly recommend (B) for v1.

objections.description: The --ease-spring cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1) has an overshoot value of 1.56 which produces a visible bounce. This directly contradicts the stated aesthetic: 'Nubank/Flighty — quick, confident, minimal overshoot. No playful/organic motion.' A 1.56 control point is objectively bouncy — that's playful motion. The snap-in animation using this curve will make cards visibly overshoot their final scale, which reads as whimsical, not premium.
objections.suggested_fix: Replace --ease-spring with a tighter curve: `cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1)` — this is expo-out with a slight snap, zero overshoot. Reserve spring-like curves only if you intentionally want bounce on a specific micro-interaction (toggle switch, etc.) and apply it as a one-off, not as a global motion class. Rename the variable to --ease-snap-out to match its actual behavior.

objections.description: Hash routing (#/) means the entire URL after the hash is invisible to the server. This is fine for static serving today, but it permanently prevents SSR, prevents backend-aware routing (e.g., web_server.py returning different preload headers per route), and produces ugly URLs (#/doc/abc123). The spec should acknowledge this as a conscious tradeoff, not just a default choice. More critically: if web_server.py already serves HTML at path-based routes (e.g., /search), switching to hash routes creates a breaking change for any bookmarked URLs.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a note confirming web_server.py does NOT currently serve route-specific HTML (it's a static file server). If it does, add redirect middleware. Also add to the migration path: 'v2 consideration: move to History API pushState routing with a web_server.py catch-all that serves index.html for all non-API, non-static paths.' This makes the hash routing an intentional v1 decision with a documented escape hatch.

objections.description: The migration path is underspecified. The existing index.html has Alpine.js bindings. The spec says the new index.html references dist/main.js. But there's no step-by-step migration plan: When does Alpine get removed? Can the old and new UIs coexist during migration? What happens to users who have the old page cached? Is there a feature flag or A/B path?
objections.suggested_fix: Add a migration sequence: Step 1: Copy current index.html to _legacy/index-alpine.html. Step 2: Create new index.html with React mount point, import map, theme script. Step 3: web_server.py serves new index.html at / — no feature flag needed since this is a full replacement. Step 4: Verify all backend API routes are hit correctly from new frontend. Step 5: Remove _legacy/ after 1 sprint of production stability. This is a big-bang migration — own it explicitly.

objections.description: --text-tertiary (#71717a) on --surface-0 (#ffffff) in light mode = 4.48:1 contrast ratio. This fails WCAG AA for normal text (requires 4.5:1). It's borderline enough to pass some tools and fail others depending on rounding. If tertiary text is used for timestamps, captions, or any text below 18px, it's a violation.
objections.suggested_fix: Darken light-mode --text-tertiary to #52525b (zinc-600, same as --text-secondary) or #636370 to achieve 4.5:1+. Alternatively, if tertiary text is only used at --font-lg (18px+), it qualifies as 'large text' and 3:1 suffices — but document this constraint explicitly so no one uses it at 12px.

objections.location: Section 4 — Component Map: SearchPresets in HomeScreen
objections.description: HomeScreen renders <SearchPresets presets onSelect>. When a preset is selected, the expected behavior is unclear: does it navigate to #/search?q=preset.query, or does it open search inline? If it navigates, the Home→Search→Results flow involves two tab switches (Home tab deactivates, Search tab activates) which may confuse users about where they 'are.' Kimi's IA should have caught this.
objections.suggested_fix: Specify: tapping a preset on Home navigates to #/search?q={preset.query} and the Search tab becomes active. SearchScreen reads the ?q= param from the hash on mount and auto-executes the search. This makes the preset a shortcut, not a duplicate search surface.

suggestions.description: Add a fetchHealthCheck(signal?) → Promise<{ok: boolean}> for GET /api/health. Use it in the Shell component on mount to detect if the backend is reachable at all, before firing individual data fetches. This differentiates 'backend is down' from 'this specific endpoint failed' and lets you show a single full-screen error instead of 4 individual ErrorStates.

suggestions.location: Section 11 — Screen Specs: Home Screen Phase 4
suggestions.description: Phase 4 says 'deferred, after Phase 3 paint.' Clarify the mechanism: use requestIdleCallback or a useEffect that fires after the Phase 3 state update commits. requestIdleCallback is the right choice here — it ensures the browser has painted the KPI/status content before kicking off lower-priority network requests.

<60KB gzipped is ambitious with React 18 (~42KB min+gz) plus app code. If esbuild bundles React (per my recommendation to drop import maps), you have ~18KB for all app code, icons, and utilities. That's achievable but tight. Add Preact as a documented fallback option via the esbuild alias flag (`--alias:react=preact/compat`) if the bundle exceeds 60KB. Preact compat is ~4KB vs React's 42KB.

Add explicit keyboard navigation spec for the TabBar: arrow-left/arrow-right to move between tabs, Enter/Space to activate, Home/End to jump to first/last tab. Follow the WAI-ARIA Tabs pattern with role='tablist', role='tab', aria-selected, and roving tabindex.

The single ErrorBoundary wrapping Shell catches render crashes but shows a full-screen error for any component failure — including a single KPIMetric throwing. Add per-screen ErrorBoundaries inside Shell so a crash in SearchScreen doesn't take down the entire app. The outer boundary becomes the last-resort fallback.
